chunk_summary: This section discusses nutrition strategies for infants and young children with chronic illnesses or immunodeficiency, including feed concentration, tube feeding, food safety, and specialized dietary approaches for conditions like CHD, HIV, and metabolic disorders.
learning_objectives: Understand nutritional modifications for infants with chronic illnesses such as CHD and chronic lung disease.
Learn food safety guidelines for immunocompromised pediatric patients.
Explore dietary management for inborn errors of metabolism like PKU and galactosemia.
Recognize the role of exclusive breastfeeding and complementary feeding in HIV-exposed or infected infants.
key_facts: Infants with CHD or chronic lung disease may require higher-calorie feeds (e.g., concentrated breast milk or formula).
Exclusive breastfeeding with maternal ART and infant prophylaxis is recommended for HIV-exposed infants to prevent mixed feeding risks.
Micronutrient supplementation is critical after 6 months for HIV-infected infants due to growth faltering risks.
Immunocompromised children should avoid raw/undercooked eggs, meats, unpasteurized dairy, and high-risk street foods.
Dietary therapy for PKU involves specialized formulas and careful complementary feeding under metabolic team guidance.
reference_quote: Exclusive breastfeeding with maternal ART and infant prophylaxis is recommended for HIV-exposed infants to prevent mixed feeding risks. (WHO HIV and infant feeding guideline update 2016)
document_key_topics: Nutrition strategies for chronic pediatric conditions
Specialized dietary management for metabolic disorders
